modules.title: Decision Trees
modules.description: Master the fundamental algorithms of data splitting. Learn visually with our interactive playground.

theory.content: A **Decision Tree** makes classifications by asking a sequential hierarchy of questions. Imagine playing "20 Questions" to identify an animal. You might ask: *"Does it have fur?"* If true, you travel down the left branch. If false, the right.

Unlike humans, algorithms don't manually guess which questions to ask. Instead, they algorithmically evaluate every possible feature and slice the dataset at points that maximize the **Gini Impurity** drop or **Information Gain**.

theory.title: Key Parameters
items: **Max Depth:** Restricts the vertical progression of the tree. Endless depth results in a model that memorizes training data perfectly (Overfitting) but cannot generalize.
**Min Samples Split:** The minimum isolated points required to justify spinning up a new branch. A tighter constraint forces broader generalization.

theory.content: You don't need to understand the underlying mathematics to wield the power of Decision Trees. You just need to understand the conceptual boundaries. When you constrain the geometry of a model, you alter its predictive universe.

quiz.question: What happens when you increase the Max Depth of a Decision Tree too much?
options: The model overfits the training data

quiz.question: What metric does a Decision Tree typically use to decide where to split?
What is the role of 'Min Samples Split'?
options: It defines the minimum data points needed to create a new branch
It controls the number of trees

modules.title: K-Means Clustering
modules.description: Discover how unsupervised algorithms group unlabeled data into meaningful clusters automatically.

theory.content: Unlike Decision Trees, **K-Means Clustering** doesn't need labeled data. It's an *unsupervised* algorithm that discovers hidden structure by grouping similar data points into K clusters without being told what those groups should be.

K-Means works through elegant simplicity: **1)** Randomly place K centroids. **2)** Assign every data point to its nearest centroid. **3)** Recalculate each centroid as the mean of its assigned points. **4)** Repeat steps 2-3 until centroids stabilize (convergence).

theory.title: Key Concepts
items: **K (Number of Clusters):** The only hyperparameter. Choosing the right K is critical — too few clusters merge distinct groups, too many fragment coherent ones.
**Centroid:** The mathematical center of a cluster. Points are always assigned to the nearest centroid by Euclidean distance.
**Inertia:** The sum of squared distances from each point to its centroid. Lower inertia means tighter clusters.

theory.content: K-Means is used everywhere: customer segmentation, image compression, anomaly detection, and even grouping galaxies. Its speed and simplicity make it one of the most deployed algorithms in production ML systems worldwide.

quiz.question: K-Means Clustering is what type of learning?
What does the 'K' in K-Means represent?
What metric does K-Means minimize?
options: Inertia (sum of squared distances to centroids)

modules.title: Neural Network Basics
modules.description: Understand the building blocks of deep learning: neurons, layers, activation functions, and forward propagation.

theory.content: A **Neural Network** is a computational architecture inspired by the biological neurons in the human brain. At its core, it's a function that maps inputs to outputs through layers of interconnected nodes, each performing a simple linear transformation followed by a non-linear activation.

A single artificial neuron computes: **output = activation(weights · inputs + bias)**. The *weights* determine how important each input is. The *bias* shifts the decision boundary. The *activation function* (like ReLU or Sigmoid) introduces non-linearity, enabling the network to learn complex patterns beyond simple lines.

theory.title: Core Building Blocks
items: **Input Layer:** Receives the raw feature data. Each node represents one feature dimension.
**Hidden Layers:** The computational engine. More layers = deeper network = ability to learn more abstract representations.
**Output Layer:** Produces the final prediction. For classification, often uses Softmax to output probabilities.
**Backpropagation:** The algorithm that computes gradients and adjusts weights to minimize prediction error, flowing backwards through the network.

theory.content: Neural Networks power everything from image recognition (CNNs) to language models (Transformers). Understanding their fundamentals — forward propagation, loss computation, and gradient descent — is the gateway to modern AI engineering.

quiz.question: What does an activation function do in a neural network?
What is backpropagation?
options: An algorithm to compute gradients and update weights

quiz.question: What does the Softmax function output?
